comments:
Summary:
This paper presents AirSim, an open-source simulator for autonomous aerial vehicles built as a plugin to Unreal Engine 4. The system emphasizes high-fidelity visual rendering and a real-time physics/sensor stack intended for hardware-in-the-loop (HITL) and learning-based development (e.g., reinforcement/imitation learning). The authors describe vehicle/environment modeling, a custom physics engine (including drag, angular drag approximations, integration via Verlet, collision handling leveraging UE4), sensor models (IMU, barometer, magnetometer, GPS), and present experiments comparing simulated and real quadrotor flights (trajectory Hausdorff distances) and sensor-level comparisons.

Strengths:
- Practical and timely: addresses a well-motivated need for simulators that narrow the sim-to-real gap for ML-driven robotics.
- Engineering contribution: integrates high-fidelity rendering (UE4) with a custom, low-latency physics and sensor stack suitable for HITL; modular API design is sensible.
- Open-source orientation and use of photogrammetry assets make the platform accessible for the community.
- The paper contains concrete implementation details (propeller force model, magnetic/pressure models, sensor noise/drift modeling) and real-world validation on a Pixhawk-equipped quadrotor, with quantitative trajectory comparisons.

Weaknesses and limitations:
- Comparisons to related simulators are qualitative and high-level. There is little quantitative head-to-head benchmarking (accuracy, performance, or developer ergonomics) versus Gazebo, RotorS, jMavSim, etc.
- Evaluation is relatively narrow: experiments focus on two simple trajectories (circle and square) and a small set of sensors. The sensor validation shows reasonable agreement for stationary cases, but in-flight IMU variance notably diverges due to unmodeled frame vibration — an acknowledged limitation. More diverse scenarios (obstacles, complex maneuvers, adverse weather/wind, degraded GPS) would strengthen claims about sim-to-real fidelity.
- Some physics approximations are coarse (tilted dipole magnetic model, box approximation for angular drag, simple wind model, simple GPS model). These are pragmatic for real-time use but should be discussed in terms of the limits they impose on transfer fidelity.
- Performance characterization is missing: CPU/GPU load, achievable simulation rates under representative scenes, latency for HITL, and scalability (multiple vehicles) are not reported.
- Presentation has small issues: repeated paragraphs/fragments and a few typographical/formatting artifacts in the provided text.

Suggestions for improvement:
- Add more quantitative comparisons to existing simulators along axes such as realism metrics, simulation speed/latency, and ease of integration with common flight stacks.
- Expand validation: more complex flight patterns, obstacle avoidance tasks, perception-driven tasks (e.g., visual odometry/SLAM performance using simulated camera imagery), and domain transfer experiments where models trained in AirSim are tested on real hardware.
- Include performance benchmarks (frame rates, physics tick rates, CPU/GPU utilization) and scalability tests (multiple vehicles, large photogrammetry-based scenes).
- Improve/extend some physics and sensor models (vibration modeling for IMU, more sophisticated wind fields, more accurate magnetic/GPS degradation modeling, camera noise and lens models) and report their impact.
- Clean up presentation and remove duplicated text; provide link(s) to the repository and usage examples/demos in the paper for reproducibility.

Overall recommendation:
This is a solid systems paper with immediate practical value to the robotics and ML-for-robotics communities. It documents a usable, extensible simulator with careful engineering trade-offs and some empirical validation. I recommend acceptance with an eye toward strengthening quantitative comparisons and broader validation in future revisions or follow-on work.
